summary:
The Afsharid Dynasty (1736–1796 AD) was a short-lived but extraordinarily dynamic Iranian dynasty founded by [[event:2447|Nader Shah]] — a Turkic chieftain from Khorasan who rose through military service to the last Safavid shahs, seized power himself, and briefly created an empire stretching from the Caucasus to the Indus River. Nader Shah was one of the 18th century's most capable military commanders: he expelled Afghan invaders from Persia, restored Safavid authority (initially), and then overthrew the Safavids and had himself crowned shah in 1736. His campaigns were relentless — he reconquered Herat, invaded the [[event:103|Mughal Empire]] and sacked Delhi (1739), taking the Peacock Throne and the Koh-i-Noor diamond, and simultaneously fought the Ottomans in the west and the Uzbeks in the north.

Nader Shah's empire was entirely personal — built on his military reputation and personal leadership, with no institutional foundations. His later years were marked by increasing paranoia: he blinded his son, executed thousands of suspected traitors, and imposed crushing taxes to finance his campaigns, alienating the Persian population. His assassination in 1747 by his own guards immediately triggered the empire's disintegration — his generals and successors fought each other while regional powers like the [[event:3239|Qajars]], the Zands, and the Afghan [[event:2451|Durranis]] (whose founder Ahmad Shah Durrani had served in Nader's army) established themselves as independent rulers. The Afsharid dynasty illustrates the fragility of personal empires built on military dominance without institutional underpinning — powerful in the moment but incapable of outlasting their founder.
